text: The assistant uses a grasper to hold tissue near the ileocecal valve, while the surgeon manipulates the terminal ileum and cecum, pushing them toward the right iliac fossa.
The surgeon uses a grasper to indicate the outboring of the ileocecal or ileocolic vessels, highlighting the site of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon begins dissection under the ileocolic or ileocecal vessel, which is positioned at the same level as the falciform ligament.
The surgeon measures and identifies a point four centimeters below the outbowing of the ileocolic vessel and four centimeters from the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) for further dissection.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to map the route towards the root of the ileocolic vessel, carefully delineating the area for further dissection.
The surgeon creates a tunnel to separate the mesorectum from the retroperitoneal structures, aiming to reach the origin of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon carefully dissects over the duodenum, ensuring that the duodenum remains unharmed during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to indicate the area where there is a risk of injuring the duodenum during dissection, highlighting the anatomical proximity and potential hazard.
The surgeon separates the duodenum and places a gauze inside the tunnel to protect it, ensuring the duodenum remains safe from injury during dissection.
The surgeon attempts to locate the root of the ileocolic vessel, with the absence of gauze making the tissue less distinct and increasing the risk of injury.
The surgeon carefully dissects the fat covering the superior mesenteric vein and the origin of the ileocolic vessel, revealing these structures for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon opens the flimsy membrane and dissects to separate the ileocolic vessel. A gauze is placed to protect the duodenum. The surgeon searches for two cylindrical structures, indicating the ileocolic vein and artery, noting that the vein is typically more superficial than the artery.
The surgeon addresses a small bleeding by performing meticulous hemostasis. An energy device with a harmonic tip is used as a dissector to carefully remove fat over the ileocolic vessel.

The surgeon continues dissection to expose the superior mesenteric vein, aiming to ligate it at the root. Emerging structures possibly include the ileocolic vein, with a visible lymph node in the area.

The surgeon performs central vessel ligation, carefully removing fat from the vessel using traction. Various energy devices, including bipolar and monopolar instruments, are utilized to assist in the dissection.

The surgeon carefully skeletonizes a bluish or whitish structure, identified as the ileocolic vein, to prepare it for clipping. The structure is exposed using precise dissection techniques, ensuring clear visibility for clip application.

The surgeon uses a harmonic instrument to dissect the posterior tissue, revealing bloodless fatty tissue. The dissection allows for clear movement of the hand from both above and below the tissue plane.
The surgeon uses a grasping instrument to seal and cut the central vessels at the root, demonstrating the technique for central vessel ligation.
The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to separate the clearly visible vein, identifying the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Clips are applied precisely at the root of the vein.
The surgeon places two clips at the root of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) after removing all surrounding fatty tissue and lymph nodes, ensuring a secure ligation.
The surgeon uses an energy sealing device on the vein, preferring this method over scissors for vein manipulation. Fatty tissue is still visible, and the artery is expected to be encountered soon.

The surgeon carefully exposes the vein before the artery, applying slow and meticulous traction to avoid over or under tension. The vein is visible from multiple angles, both from above and below, ensuring clear exposure for the procedure.
The surgeon skeletonizes the origin of the ileocolic artery, using a right-angle instrument to encircle the artery, with the tip of the instrument visible.
The surgeon creates a window by dissecting posteriorly, preparing to apply clips to secure the vessel. The dissection appears sufficient for clip placement.
At the root of the ileocolic artery, the surgeon releases traction to prevent tension before applying the first clip to the vessel, ensuring it does not tear and cause bleeding.

The surgeon places two proximal clips on the vessel, ensuring they are positioned slightly away from the vessel to facilitate control in case of bleeding. The surgeon then uses scissors to cut the vessel flush with the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon places clips on the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the ileocolic vein, then proceeds to cut the ileocolic artery, revealing the lumen of the artery.
The surgeon places two clips on the ileocolic artery, first positioning the clip away from the vessel's origin, followed by the clip flush with the vessel.
